title: Data & Analytics for AI
subtitle: From raw data to AI-ready insights — pipelines, pandas, visualization, and data quality
heroTitle: Data & Analytics for AI

objectives.title: Understand the Data Pipeline
objectives.description: Trace how data flows from collection to cleaning to analysis to AI training. Know what happens at each stage and why it matters.

objectives.title: Analyze Real Business Data with pandas
objectives.description: Load, explore, clean, and transform a real-world dataset in Google Colab using Python's most popular data library.

objectives.title: Visualize Data for Decision-Making
objectives.description: Create bar charts, line plots, and scatter plots with matplotlib. Turn numbers into stories that drive business action.

objectives.title: Connect Data to Your AI Product
objectives.description: Understand how data quality directly impacts AI performance. Define the data strategy for the product you built in Session 4.

agenda.title: Part 1: Data is the New Oil (But Crude Oil is Useless)
agenda.description: Every AI product you admire runs on data. But raw data is messy, incomplete, and often wrong. Here's how to think about it.
bulletPoints: Opening hook: 'Your Session 4 product is smart. But without data, it's guessing.' Show a model trained on clean vs dirty data.
The Data Pipeline: Collection → Cleaning → Analysis → AI Training → Prediction
Real examples: Netflix recommendations (viewing data), Spotify Wrapped (listening data), Uber surge pricing (ride data)
The 80/20 rule: Data scientists spend 80% of their time cleaning data, 20% on modeling
GARBAGE IN, GARBAGE OUT: Amazon's hiring AI trained on biased data → biased results

agenda.title: Part 2: Meet pandas — Your Data Swiss Army Knife
agenda.description: pandas is Python's superpower for data. If Excel is a bicycle, pandas is a Tesla.
bulletPoints: What is pandas? DataFrames = spreadsheets in code. Same concept, infinite power.
LIVE DEMO: Load a CSV of 10,000 Airbnb listings in 1 line of code
The Big 5 operations: .head(), .describe(), .shape, .info(), .value_counts()
Filtering: 'Show me all listings under $100 in Manhattan' — one line of code
ACTION: Open the Colab notebook now. Run Cell 1 while I talk.

agenda.title: Part 3: Hands-On Data Exploration Lab
agenda.description: Get your hands dirty with real data. Load it, explore it, clean it, question it.
bulletPoints: 5:35-5:50 — EXPLORE: Load the dataset, run .describe(), find the most expensive listing, count by neighborhood
5:50-6:00 — CLEAN: Find missing values, handle outliers (a $10,000/night listing?), fix data types
6:00-6:10 — ANALYZE: Group by neighborhood, calculate averages, find patterns, answer 3 business questions
6:10-6:15 — SHARE: Tell your neighbor the most surprising thing you found in the data

agenda.title: Break
agenda.description: Stretch, grab coffee, think about what patterns you spotted.

agenda.title: Part 4: Data Visualization — Numbers Tell, Charts Sell
agenda.description: A CEO won't read your DataFrame. They'll look at your chart for 3 seconds. Make those seconds count.
bulletPoints: Why visualize? Anscombe's Quartet — 4 datasets with identical statistics, wildly different shapes
The Big 3 chart types: Bar (comparison), Line (trends), Scatter (relationships)
LIVE DEMO: Build 3 charts from our Airbnb data in matplotlib
Chart crimes: 3D pie charts, truncated axes, misleading scales — don't be that person
COLOR & LABELS: A chart without labels is just modern art. Always title, label, annotate.

agenda.title: Part 5: Visualization Workshop
agenda.description: Build 4 charts that tell a data story. Make them presentation-ready.
bulletPoints: Chart 1: Bar chart — Average price by neighborhood (which area is most expensive?)
Chart 2: Histogram — Price distribution (where do most listings fall?)
Chart 3: Scatter plot — Price vs number of reviews (does cheaper = more popular?)
Chart 4: YOUR CHOICE — Create a chart that answers a question YOU have about the data
Bonus: Add titles, colors, and annotations to make your charts boardroom-ready

agenda.title: Part 6: Data Strategy for YOUR AI Product
agenda.description: Connect everything back to the product you built in Session 4. What data does YOUR product need?
bulletPoints: CLASS DISCUSSION: 'What data would your Session 4 product need to get smarter over time?'
The Data Flywheel: More users → more data → better AI → more users (Netflix, TikTok, Google)
3 questions every AI product must answer: What data? How to collect it? How to keep it clean?
Privacy & Ethics: What data SHOULDN'T you collect? GDPR, consent, and trust
Quick exercise: Write a 3-sentence data strategy for your product

agenda.title: Part 7: Wrap-Up + Homework
agenda.description: You went from raw CSV to clean analysis to beautiful charts to AI-ready data strategy.
bulletPoints: Recap: Collection → Cleaning → Analysis → Visualization → AI Training. You did all 5 today.
Key insight: The best AI model in the world fails on bad data. Data quality IS product quality.
Homework preview: Kaggle dataset analysis, visualization portfolio, data strategy document
Next week: Computer Vision — how machines SEE the world. Bring images you want to classify!

activities.title: Data Exploration Lab
activities.description: Load a real Airbnb dataset and uncover business insights using pandas in Google Colab:

steps.title: Load & Inspect (15 min)
steps.description: Import the CSV into a DataFrame. Run .head(), .shape, .describe(), and .info() to understand what you're working with.

steps.title: Clean the Mess (10 min)
steps.description: Find missing values with .isnull().sum(). Handle outliers — is that $10,000/night listing real or a typo? Fix data types.

steps.title: Analyze & Answer (10 min)
steps.description: Use .groupby() and .mean() to answer: Which neighborhood is most expensive? Which has the most listings? What's the average price?

steps.title: Share Your Finding (5 min)
steps.description: Tell your neighbor the most surprising insight you discovered. Did anything challenge your assumptions?

codeBlock.code:
import pandas as pd

# Load 10,000 Airbnb listings in ONE line
df = pd.read_csv('airbnb_nyc.csv')

# Instant overview
print(f"Rows: {df.shape[0]:,}  |  Columns: {df.shape[1]}")
print(f"Average price: ${df['price'].mean():.2f}")
print(f"Missing values:\n{df.isnull().sum()}")

# Filter: Manhattan listings under $150
cheap_manhattan = df[(df['neighbourhood_group'] == 'Manhattan') 
                     & (df['price'] < 150)]
print(f"\nAffordable Manhattan stays: {len(cheap_manhattan):,}")

activities.title: Visualization Workshop
activities.description: Build 4 presentation-ready charts from your cleaned data using matplotlib:

steps.title: Bar Chart — Price by Neighborhood (5 min)
steps.description: Create a horizontal bar chart comparing average prices across NYC neighborhoods. Add labels, colors, and a title.

steps.title: Histogram — Price Distribution (5 min)
steps.description: Plot the distribution of listing prices. Where do most listings fall? Identify the long tail of luxury listings.

steps.title: Scatter Plot — Price vs Reviews (5 min)
steps.description: Plot price against number of reviews. Does cheaper mean more popular? Look for the relationship.

steps.title: Your Choice Chart (10 min)
steps.description: Create a chart that answers YOUR question about the data. Make it boardroom-ready with title, labels, and annotations.

codeBlock.code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Bar chart: Average price by neighborhood
avg_price = df.groupby('neighbourhood_group')['price'].mean().sort_values()

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
avg_price.plot(kind='barh', color='#6366f1')
plt.title('Average Airbnb Price by NYC Neighborhood', fontsize=16)
plt.xlabel('Average Price ($)')
plt.ylabel('')
for i, v in enumerate(avg_price):
    plt.text(v + 2, i, f'${v:.0f}', va='center')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

activities.title: Data Strategy for Your AI Product
activities.description: Connect data concepts to the product you built in Session 4. Define what data YOUR product needs:

steps.title: Identify Your Data (3 min)
steps.description: What data does your Session 4 product need? User inputs, behavior logs, external APIs? List at least 5 data points.

steps.title: Map the Pipeline (3 min)
steps.description: How would you collect, clean, and store this data? Sketch the flow: source → ingestion → storage → analysis → AI model.

steps.title: Address Quality & Ethics (3 min)
steps.description: What could go wrong? Biased data? Missing values? Privacy concerns? What data should you NOT collect?

steps.title: Write Your Data Strategy (3 min)
steps.description: 3 sentences: What data you need, how you'll collect it, and how it makes your product smarter over time.

takeaways.text: Data is the fuel, not the car. The best AI model in the world fails on bad data. You learned the full pipeline: collection, cleaning, analysis, visualization, training.
pandas turns spreadsheets into superpowers. One line to load 10,000 rows. One line to filter. One line to group. You did in seconds what takes hours in Excel.
Charts tell stories that numbers can't. Anscombe's Quartet proved it — identical statistics, totally different data. Always visualize before you model.
Garbage in, garbage out. Amazon's biased hiring AI, misleading charts, dirty datasets — data quality is not optional. It IS the product.
The Data Flywheel drives every great AI product. More users generate more data, which trains better models, which attract more users. Your Session 4 product needs this loop.

homework.label: Analyze:
homework.text: Find a dataset on Kaggle relevant to your Session 4 product. Load it in Colab, clean it, and write 5 key findings. Include your code.

homework.label: Visualize:
homework.text: Create 3 polished charts from your chosen dataset. Each chart should answer a specific business question. Add titles, labels, and annotations.

homework.label: Strategize:
homework.text: Write a 1-page data strategy for your AI product: what data you need, how to collect it, how to ensure quality, and what ethical guardrails to set.

homework.label: Explore:
homework.text: Read the pandas Getting Started guide. Try 3 operations we didn't cover in class (e.g., .merge(), .pivot_table(), .apply()). Share what you learned.

nextSession.description: How machines see the world — image recognition, object detection, and building vision-powered business applications.
